perturbation.original_text: This Agreement will commence on the Signature D ate and shall remain binding and in force for a period of five years thereafter or such longer period as the Parties may agree to in writing ("Term ").
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement will commence on the Signature Date and shall remain binding and in force for a period of fifty years thereafter or such longer period as the Parties may agree to in writing ("Term ").
perturbation.explanation: Extending the confidentiality term to 50 years may be considered unreasonably long and could be challenged as an unenforceable restraint of trade under South African law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Restraint of Trade Doctrine under South African Common Law
perturbation.law_citation: Case Law: Magna Alloys and Research (SA) (Pty) Ltd v Ellis 1984 (4) SA 874 (A)
perturbation.law_explanation: South African courts assess the reasonableness of restraint of trade agreements based on factors like duration and geographical scope. A 50-year confidentiality clause is likely to be deemed unreasonable and unenforceable, especially if it unduly restricts competition or an individual's ability to earn a livelihood. The Magna Alloys case established key principles for evaluating such restraints.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: GM's NHTSA Petition For Cars Without Steering Wheels Open For Comments - Rail, Road & Cycling - United States ARTICLE 29 March 2019 GM's NHTSA Petition For Cars Without Steering Wheels Open For Comments FL Foley & Lardner More Contributor Foley & Lardner LLP looks beyond the law to focus on the constantly evolving demands facing our clients and their industries. With over 1,100 lawyers in 24 offices across the United States, Mexico, Europe and Asia, Foley approaches client service by first understanding our clients’ priorities, objectives and challenges. We work hard to understand our clients’ issues and forge long-term relationships with them to help achieve successful outcomes and solve their legal issues through practical business advice and cutting-edge legal insight. Our clients view us as trusted business advisors because we understand that great legal service is only valuable if it is relevant, practical and beneficial to their businesses. Explore Firm Details On March 19, 2019, NHTSA published General Motors' petition seeking a temporary exemption in order to field a fleet of its fourth generation Cruise AV vehicles without traditional vehicle controls. United States Transport Foley & Lardner Your Author LinkedIn Connections To print this article, all you need is to be registered or login on Mondaq.com. On March 19, 2019, NHTSA published General Motors' petition seeking a temporary exemption in order to field a fleet of its fourth generation Cruise AV vehicles without traditional vehicle controls. When the vehicle was first announced by GM in early 2018, images depicted a Chevy Bolt interior, absent a steering wheel, pedals and mirrors, which are required under current U.S. vehicle regulations. Dan Amman, former president of GM and now-CEO of GM subsidiary, Cruise Automation, quipped at the time that "a car without a steering wheel can't have a steering wheel airbag... What we can do is put the equivalent of the passenger side airbag on that side as well. So it's to meet the standards but meet them in a way that's different than what's exactly prescribed, and that's what the petition seeks to get approval for." Amman's comments, however, gloss over the real significance of the exemption being sought, which is that GM is intent on producing a vehicle in which "human occupants in GM's [zero-emission autonomous vehicle or] ZEAV will not have access to vehicle driving controls." The petition rightly concludes that "[w]ith the ADS as the driver, there is no need for features designed to interface with a human driver, such as manual human driver controls (e.g., steering wheel, brake pedal and accelerator pedal), human‐driver‐specific information systems (e.g., telltales and indicator lamps), human‐driver‐oriented visibility features (e.g., rearview mirrors), or human‐driver‐specific occupant protection (e.g., steering‐wheel‐mounted airbag)." However, the real question that should be reviewed in the petition is not whether human-driver-based requirements should be exempted if there is no human driver, which appears quite logical, but rather, if we should effectively forbid human intervention in the first place by removing the only means of manual control from the vehicle. The petition makes a case that 94 percent of vehicle crashes are attributable to human error in support of its need to replace human drivers with its ADS. GM also hints at its plans for implementing its Cruise AV into commerce by describing conditions under which NHTSA has provided favorable variances: Manufacturer would hold title to the vehicles; Vehicles would be operated primarily in "urban environments" and at "low urban speeds"; Vehicles would have "low operating speeds" and be deployed in "urban use"; Speed "will not exceed 40 mph"; and It is unlikely that the vehicles would be driven in "rain, snow, or winter months." GM makes it clear that it intends to maintain title and control over its Cruise AV vehicles during the two-year duration of the exempt
perturbation.justification: The original contract specifies a five-year confidentiality term, while the changed text extends it to fifty years. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation both suggest that a 50-year confidentiality clause is likely to be deemed unreasonable and unenforceable under South African law, particularly concerning restraint of trade. The change introduces a potentially unenforceable and unreasonable term that could unduly restrict competition or an individual's ability to earn a livelihood. Snippet 2 doesn't directly address confidentiality terms, but it does mention NHTSA, General Motors, and Cruise AV, and implies the government's power to regulate trade. Since the change violates a legal standard, a contradiction exists.

perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party shall comply with any requests in terms of 6.1 and/or 6.2 above within five "Business Days " (being any day other than a Saturday, Sunday or official public holiday in the Republic of South Africa) of the date of such a request.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party shall comply with any requests in terms of 6.1 and/or 6.2 above within twenty "Business Days " (being any day other than a Saturday, Sunday or official public holiday in the Republic of South Africa) of the date of such a request.
perturbation.explanation: Extending the response time to 20 business days for returning or destroying confidential information may be considered commercially unreasonable and could potentially prejudice the Disclosing Party's ability to protect its trade secrets effectively.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Protection of Personal Information Act (POPIA), Act No. 4 of 2013 (South Africa)
perturbation.law_explanation: While POPIA primarily concerns personal information, its underlying principles emphasize the responsible and timely handling of information. Section 14(2) requires data to be destroyed or deleted as soon as reasonably practicable. A lengthy 20-business-day period could be seen as conflicting with the 'reasonably practicable' requirement, especially if the confidential information includes personal data regulated by POPIA.
perturbation.justification: The change from five to twenty business days introduces an inconsistency with the principle of 'reasonably practicable' data handling as suggested in the Law Explanation. While POPIA is not directly quoted in the scraped snippets, the Law Explanation indicates that a 20-business-day period for responding to requests could be seen as conflicting with the 'reasonably practicable' requirement for destroying or deleting data, especially personal data. The change weakens the original obligation by significantly extending the response timeframe, thus undermining the timely handling of information and creating a potential legal issue.

perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party agrees that , save as contemplated in any definitive transaction agreement concluded between the Parties for purposes of the Transaction, it will not, for a period of 24 months a fter the Signature Date, directly or indirectly, and on its own behalf or on behalf of any other person, encourage, entice, induce or persuade any person - 7.1 who is, as at the Signature Date or during the course of the Parties discussions relating to the Pu rpose, in the employ of the Disclosing Party or its Affiliates ; and 7.2 with whom the Receiving Party has had or may have had any contact in relation to the Confidential Information or whose name and/or any other details are included in the Confidential I nformation, to terminate such employment, nor shall it encourage or support any of its Affiliates to do so.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party agrees that , save as contemplated in any definitive transaction agreement concluded between the Parties for purposes of the Transaction, it will not, for a period of 60 months a fter the Signature Date, directly or indirectly, and on its own behalf or on behalf of any other person, encourage, entice, induce or persuade any person - 7.1 who is, as at the Signature Date or during the course of the Parties discussions relating to the Pu rpose, in the employ of the Disclosing Party or its Affiliates ; and 7.2 with whom the Receiving Party has had or may have had any contact in relation to the Confidential Information or whose name and/or any other details are included in the Confidential I nformation, to terminate such employment, nor shall it encourage or support any of its Affiliates to do so.
perturbation.explanation: Extending the non-solicitation period to 60 months (5 years) may be deemed an unreasonable restraint of trade under South African law, potentially hindering employee mobility and competition.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Restraint of Trade Doctrine under South African Common Law
perturbation.law_citation: Case Law: Reddy v Siemens Telecommunications (Pty) Ltd 2007 (2) SA 486 (SCA)
perturbation.law_explanation: South African courts scrutinize the reasonableness of restraint of trade clauses, considering factors such as the duration and scope of the restraint. A 5-year non-solicitation clause is a long duration and could be seen as unduly restrictive, especially if the employee's skills are not highly specialized or the confidential information is not particularly sensitive. The Reddy v Siemens case provides further guidance on evaluating the reasonableness of such restraints.

perturbation.justification: The change extends the non-solicitation period from 24 months to 60 months. According to the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation, this extended period may be deemed an unreasonable restraint of trade under South African law, potentially hindering employee mobility and competition, and is likely to be viewed as unduly restrictive. Although Scraped Law Snippet 1 is not about South African Law, the Law Explanation references the Reddy v Siemens case as providing guidance on evaluating the reasonableness of such restraints. The change creates a contradiction because an unreasonable restraint of trade would likely be unenforceable, directly conflicting with the original contract's intent to enforce a non-solicitation agreement.

perturbation.original_text: Any notice given by either Party to the other ("Addressee ") which is - 12.3.1 delivered by hand between the hours of 09:00 and 17:00 on any Business Day to the Addressee 's physical Address shall be deemed to have been received by the Addressee at the time of del ivery; 12.3.2 successfully transmitted by facsimile to the Addressee's facsimile Address shall be deemed (unless the contrary is proved by the Addressee) to have been received by the Addressee on the day immediately succeeding the date of successful transmission thereof; and 12.3.3 sent to the chosen email address of the Addressee shall be deemed, failing any administrator or mail server errors, to have been received by the Addressee on the Business Day immediately following the date of sending, unless the Addressee proves that the email was not delivered to their email system or that the email was incapable of being retrieved, read or otherw ise processed by the Addressee 's system.
perturbation.changed_text: Any notice given by either Party to the other ("Addressee ") which is - 12.3.1 delivered by hand between the hours of 09:00 and 17:00 on any Business Day to the Addressee 's physical Address shall be deemed to have been received by the Addressee three days after delivery; 12.3.2 successfully transmitted by facsimile to the Addressee's facsimile Address shall be deemed (unless the contrary is proved by the Addressee) to have been received by the Addressee five days succeeding the date of successful transmission thereof; and 12.3.3 sent to the chosen email address of the Addressee shall be deemed, failing any administrator or mail server errors, to have been received by the Addressee ten days following the date of sending, unless the Addressee proves that the email was not delivered to their email system or that the email was incapable of being retrieved, read or otherw ise processed by the Addressee 's system.
perturbation.explanation: Delaying the deemed receipt of notices significantly could be considered an unreasonable contractual term, especially in business rescue proceedings where time is of the essence. This could prejudice a party's ability to respond to critical notices within legally mandated timeframes.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Companies Act 71 of 2008 (South Africa) - Business Rescue Provisions
perturbation.law_citation: Section 129 - 154 of the Companies Act 71 of 2008
perturbation.law_explanation: The Companies Act outlines specific timeframes and procedures for business rescue. Delaying the deemed receipt of notices related to business rescue proceedings could impede the efficiency and effectiveness of the process, potentially prejudicing stakeholders and undermining the objectives of business rescue. While not directly contradicting a specific section about notice periods, the delay undermines the spirit and intention of the Act's provisions for time-sensitive actions during business rescue.
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces significantly delayed notice periods (3, 5, and 10 days respectively) for various communication methods, while the original text specified immediate or next-day receipt. The Perturbation Explanation highlights that such delays could be unreasonable, especially in time-sensitive business rescue proceedings. The Law Explanation further argues that these delays undermine the efficiency and effectiveness of business rescue, potentially prejudicing stakeholders and undermining the Act's objectives. This falls under the definition of 'Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction' as the changed text creates time-sensitive obligations that do not align with the legal intention of timely business rescue proceedings. Without the snippets provided, this determination relies on the perturbation and law explanations and the direct comparison of the original and changed text. The delayed time frames in the changed text directly contradict the implicit need for prompt action within legally mandated timeframes, particularly in business rescue proceedings.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall in all respects be governed by the laws of the Republic of South Africa .
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall in all respects be governed by the laws of the State of Delaware, USA.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law to Delaware law despite the parties and subject matter being based in South Africa could be problematic. It may violate principles of conflict of laws and raise enforceability issues in South African courts.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Conflict of Laws Principles under South African Private International Law
perturbation.law_citation: Case Law: Laconian Maritime Enterprises Ltd v Agromar Lines
perturbation.law_explanation: South African courts apply conflict of laws principles to determine the appropriate governing law. Factors such as the place of contract formation, the place of performance, and the domicile of the parties are considered. Choosing Delaware law with no connection to the contract or parties could be viewed as an attempt to evade South African law and may not be upheld by South African courts. The case Laconian Maritime Enterprises Ltd v Agromar Lines established principles guiding the application of foreign law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: The Commissioner for the South African Revenue Service v Airports Company for South Africa (785/2021) [2022] ZASCA 132; 2023 (2) SA 506 (SCA); 85 SATC 1 (7 October 2022) About SAFLII Databases Search Terms of Use RSS Feeds South Africa: Supreme Court of Appeal You are here: SAFLII >> Databases >> South Africa: Supreme Court of Appeal >> 2022 >> [2022] ZASCA 132 | Noteup | LawCite The Commissioner for the South African Revenue Service v Airports Company for South Africa (785/2021) [2022] ZASCA 132; 2023 (2) SA 506 (SCA); 85 SATC 1 (7 October 2022) Download original files PDF format RTF format THE SUPREME COURT OF APPEAL OF SOUTH AFRICA JUDGMENT Reportable Case no: 785/2021 In the matter between: THE COMMISSIONER FOR THE SOUTH AFRICAN REVENUE SERVICE APPELLANT and AIRPORTS COMPANY SOUTH AFRICA RESPONDENT Neutral citation: The Commissioner for the South African Revenue Service v Airports Company for South Africa (Case no 785/2021) [2022] ZASCA 132 (7 October 2022) Coram: PONNAN and HUGHES JJA and BASSON, WEINER and WINDELL AJJA Heard : 8 September 2022 Delivered : 7 October 2022 Summary: Tax Administration Act 28 of 2011 – application for amendment to objection against an additional assessment - no procedure in the Act for amendment of an objection - taxpayer not entitled to amendment in terms of Uniform rule 28(1) , read with rule 42(1) , of the tax court rules. ORDER On appeal from: Tax Court, Johannesburg (Ally AJ sitting as court of first instance): 1 The appeal is upheld with costs. 2 The order of the tax court is set aside and replaced with the following: ‘ The application is dismissed’. JUDGMENT WINDELL AJA (PONNAN and HUGHES JJA, WEINER and BASSON AJJA concurring) [1] Two questions arise in this appeal. First, is it permissible to amend the grounds of objection against an additional assessment issued by the appellant, the Commissioner for the South African Revenue Service (SARS), after the expiry of the periods prescribed in the tax court rules? [1] Second, is such an order appealable? Although the second is the logically anterior question, I shall first consider the former, which as shall presently become apparent is necessarily dispositive of the latter. [2] The tax court (Ally AJ) held that the taxpayer, Airports Company South Africa (the taxpayer), is permitted, under rule 42(1) of the tax court rules (rule 42(1)), read with rule 28(1) of the Uniform Rules of Court (Uniform rule 28), to amend its objection against an additional assessment issued by SARS on 30 March 2016 in respect of the taxpayer’s 2011 year of assessment. The appeal is with the leave of the tax court. Background facts [3] During December 2015 to February 2016, SARS conducted an income tax audit in respect of the taxpayer’s 2011 year of assessment. SARS issued a Letter of Audit findings on 8 February 2016. The taxpayer was advised that SARS intended to, inter alia: (a) disallow a deduction claimed by the taxpayer in respect of corporate social investment (CSI) expenditure in terms of s 11(a), read with s 23(g), of the Income Tax Act 58 of 1962 (the Act); (b) disallow an allowance claimed by the taxpayer in terms of s 13quin of the Act; (c) disallow an allowance claimed by the taxpayer in terms of s 12F of the Act; and (d) impose understatement penalties (USPs) in terms of the Tax Administration Act 28 of 2011 (TAA). [4] In a letter dated 8 March 2016, the taxpayer, through its erstwhile attorneys, addressed the adjustments in relation to (a) and (b) above and sought an extension to deal with (c). A week later, on 15 March 2016, the taxpayer addressed a further letter to SARS in which it indicated that it ‘deemed it appropriate to concede to the findings made by SARS in the Letter of Findings in respect of the application of section 12F’ . On 30 March 2016, SARS issued a Finalisation of Audit letter in respect of the taxpayer’s 2011 year of assessment and issued an additional assessment. It disallowed the CSI expenditure, as well as the s 13 quin and s
perturbation.justification: The change from South African law to Delaware law introduces a significant legal contradiction. The LLM's law explanation states that South African courts consider factors like the place of contract formation, performance, and domicile when determining governing law. Choosing Delaware law, with no apparent connection to the contract or parties based in South Africa, could be seen as an attempt to evade South African law. The altered text undermines the application of established conflict of laws principles, creating enforceability issues. While neither snippet provides a specific statute prohibiting this change, the principle from the LLM explanation, combined with the perturbation explanation, establishes a conflict based on established legal principles in South Africa. The change is more than a minor adjustment; it's a fundamental shift that could nullify the agreement's enforceability in South Africa.
